name: Epic — Definition of Ready
description: 7-criterion checklist confirming an epic is fundable, measurable, and decomposable before features are spun up against it.

criteria.name: Strategic Outcome Stated as a Measurable Change
criteria.description: The outcome is expressed as 'something is true after that wasn't true before' — with a baseline and a target.
criteria.why: Without a measurable outcome, the portfolio can't tell if the epic was successful.
criteria.fix: State the outcome as: '[metric] moves from [baseline] to [target] by [date].' Avoid output-led language ('build the AUS API').
criteria.good_example: 80% of conforming SF loans receive a lender commitment same-day, up from 35% baseline, by end of Q4.

criteria.name: Business Sponsor Named and Engaged
criteria.description: An accountable executive who funds and owns the outcome is named.
criteria.why: Sponsor-less epics drift and stall when trade-offs need a decision.
criteria.fix: Name the accountable executive (title + name) who funds the work and reviews progress at portfolio level.
criteria.good_example: Sponsor: SVP, SF Acquisitions — J. Lee. Engaged via monthly portfolio review.

criteria.name: Lines of Business and Lifecycle Stages Identified
criteria.description: Which LoBs (SF / MF / CapMkts) and which of the 10 lifecycle stages the epic touches are explicitly listed.
criteria.why: Cross-LoB and cross-stage epics need explicit coordination — implicit scope causes mid-stream surprises.
criteria.fix: List the LoBs (any combination of SF / MF / CapMkts) and the lifecycle stages (Acquisition, UW, Pricing & Commitments, Loan Boarding, Servicing, Default Mgmt, Securitization, Investor Reporting, CapMkts Ops, Risk & Compliance) the epic touches.
criteria.good_example: LoB: Single Family. Lifecycle stages: Acquisition → Underwriting → Pricing & Commitments.

criteria.name: Success Metrics Agreed with Finance and Risk
criteria.description: 1-3 metrics with current baseline and target, signed off by finance and risk partners.
criteria.why: Metrics not pre-agreed with finance/risk get challenged at delivery time and the epic's success becomes contestable.
criteria.fix: Document 1-3 metrics with baselines and targets and capture sign-off from finance and risk before kicking off features.
criteria.good_example: % same-day commits (baseline 35%, target 80%); Lender NPS (+15 points); Manual review volume (-50%). Signed off by Finance (M. Tan) and Risk (P. Singh) on 2026-04-28.

criteria.name: Capacity Envelope Confirmed at Portfolio Review
criteria.description: Approximate quarters of effort and number of squads expected to contribute have been agreed at portfolio review.
criteria.why: Without a capacity envelope, the portfolio overcommits and competing epics stall each other.
criteria.fix: State the rough capacity ask (quarters × squads) and confirm it has been accepted at portfolio review.
criteria.good_example: Capacity: 2 quarters × 3 squads (1 SF Origination, 1 Platform, 1 Risk Reporting). Confirmed at Q3 portfolio review.

criteria.name: Initial Decomposition Into Features Begun
criteria.description: Top-level feature list is drafted, even if rough — proves the epic is decomposable.
criteria.why: Epics that can't be decomposed are too big to plan and tend to absorb scope indefinitely.
criteria.fix: Draft the initial feature list (titles + capability statements). 3-7 features is typical; >10 suggests the epic should be split.
criteria.good_example: Features: 1) Real-Time AUS Decision API · 2) Lender Onboarding Flow · 3) Same-Day Commitment Letter Generation · 4) Lender Self-Service Portal.

criteria.name: Top 3 Risks Surfaced With Named Owners
criteria.description: The three biggest risks to the epic's success are named, each with a named owner and a mitigation approach.
criteria.why: Risks surfaced late become program issues; risks surfaced early become managed work.
criteria.fix: Document the top 3 risks (e.g. data dependency slip, regulatory change, vendor delay) with a named owner and mitigation for each.
criteria.good_example: R1: EDW team capacity slip — owner: A. Sharma — mitigation: pre-book Q4 sprints. R2: FHFA rule change in Q4 — owner: C. Mehta — mitigation: monthly check-in with regulator liaison. R3: Lender adoption < 30% — owner: J. Lee — mitigation: partner with top 3 lenders before GA.
